subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear presentation of raw and processed data tables and multiple graphs
Key theoretical equations presented correctly with defined variables
Initial uncertainty for length stated and propagated to derived quantities
weaknesses: Numerous calculation and typographical errors in processed data (miscomputed Î» and f values)
Inconsistent significant figures, garbled tables, and spelling mistakes reduce precision
Incomplete uncertainty analysis (ignored calibration and temperature uncertainties)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is generally clear but lacks precision (band 3), uncertainties are considered but with significant omissions (band 3), and data processing shows major inaccuracies (band 2). Overall performance aligns with band 3 for data analysis, so a score of 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Research question situated in a very specific and appropriate context (real-world resonance examples, clear linkage to apparatus)
Clear statement of independent and dependent variables with theoretical grounding
Step-by-step method with annotated photographs supports reproducibility
weaknesses: Ambiguity in describing frequency increments (â200 intervalsâ) hinders exact reproduction
End-correction mentioned but not operationalised or quantified
Duplicate descriptions of dependent vs independent variables and unclear control of speed of sound
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Contextualisation is consistently specific and appropriate (band 5â6), the method allows reproduction overall but with ambiguities (band 3â4), and methodological considerations are described but not fully explained (band 3). On balance, the submission demonstrates strong contextual framing and largely reproducible design, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion articulates the inverse Lâf relationship with reference to specific data pairs and fitted curve
Comparison to accepted theory of speed of sound and pipe resonance is relevant
Links conclusions back to theoretical framework f=nv/4L
weaknesses: Misquotation of a theoretical â860 Hzâ value not derived from the data set
No quantitative error percentages or detailed discussion of anomalies
Limited treatment of end-correction literature and quantitative agreement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clearly linked to analysis and theory (band 3â4) and contains some relevant scientific context comparison (band 3â4). The justified relationship and appropriate references to theory push it toward the top of band 3â4, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses identified (subjective detection, high-n uncertainty) with qualitative impact discussion
Realistic and relevant improvements proposed (Fourier analysis, decibel meter) linked to observed limitations
Acknowledgement of both systematic and random error sources
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative analysis of the relative impact of each limitation
Omission of discussion on instrument calibration and measurement resolution
Improvements described without detailed explanation of error reduction magnitude
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes specific weaknesses and limitations (band 3â4) and proposes realistic, relevant improvements (band 3â4). While deeper quantitative impact analysis is missing, the solid performance throughout the evaluation criterion justifies a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student situates the research question firmly within the well-defined context of resonance in a one-end-open tube, linking it to real-world examples (Tacoma Narrows, musical acoustics) and to the specific apparatus that will be used.  The description consistently narrows from waves in general to this particular physical system, demonstrating an appropriate and specific context rather than a merely broad one.

Instrumental uncertainty for length is stated and propagated to Î» and f for sample points; error bars are shown on one summary graph.  Yet full propagation for every data point is absent, calibration uncertainty for frequency is ignored, and temperature uncertainty is not treated, leaving significant gaps.  This constitutes consideration with omissions.

Relevant variables are identified and most are given a rationale and control strategy; nonetheless several controls are superficial (speed of sound listed as a âcontrolled variableâ, yet only calculated post-hoc) and the planned data set (9â10 frequencies, two trials) is only just sufficient.  The student describes rather than fully explains how the considerations ensure adequacy of data.

Specific weaknesses are namedâsubjective detection of resonance, increased uncertainty at high nâand their impact on data quality is discussed qualitatively, showing more than a generic list but not a detailed quantitative impact analysis.  This fits the âdescribes specific weaknessesâ level.

The step-by-step method, list of materials and annotated photographs would allow most students to reproduce the set-up; however, several ambiguities remainâe.g. the phrase âspecific frequencies of 200 intervalsâ is unclear, the procedure for detecting resonance (simply âlisten for louder soundâ) is subjective, and end-correction is mentioned but not operationalised.  These omissions mean reproduction is possible but with some uncertainty.

Key calculations (conversion of cm to m, Î» = 4L/n, v = fÎ») are performed, but numerous numerical and typographical errors appear (e.g. Î» mis-computed with factor 14, identical speeds listed for very different cases).  These inaccuracies and inconsistencies indicate only partial success in processing the data.

Improvements such as automated data logging and Fourier analysis are relevant and linked to identified weaknesses, but the explanation of how they would quantitatively reduce error is limited.  The ideas are described with clear relevance, meeting the descriptor for band 3-4 rather than a thorough explanation.

Raw and processed tables, multiple graphs and an uncertainty plot are provided, so the communication is generally clear; however, duplicated/garbled tables, inconsistent significant figures and spelling errors (e.g. âlenghtâ) reduce precision.  Hence communication is clear OR precise but not both.

The conclusion is related to the textbook equation f = nv/4L and to accepted speed-of-sound theory; comparisons are relevant but brief, with no quantitative discussion of percentage agreement or end-correction literature.  Thus there is some relevant comparison but not a fully justified one.

The student states an inverse relationship and cites specific data pairs and the fitted curve to justify it, so the conclusion is linked to analysis; however, misquoted âcalculatedâ value of 860 Hz (does not stem from data set) and limited treatment of anomalies mean the justification is not fully consistent.  This aligns with a descriptive but not wholly secure conclusion.

annotations.comment: Several Î» and f values in the processed data table are miscomputed or inconsistent (e.g. Î»=0.32 m at 800 Hz gives fâ1075 Hz, not 1073.53); recheck calculations for accuracy.
Graph 1 caption uses f = 344Â·n/41 but theoretical relationship is f = nv/(4L); correct the model equation in the legend to match your tube length parameter.

The introduction effectively situates the research within real-world phenomena and bridges from general wave concepts to resonance in pipes, demonstrating strong contextualization for the research question.
The research question is clearly stated with independent and dependent variables and theoretical principles of wave interference, providing a focused scope for the investigation.
The background section mentions end-correction but lacks an operational definition or quantitative adjustment; clarify how you will apply and calculate this correction.
Equations (1) and (2) are correctly presented and variables are defined, supporting clear communication of the theoretical framework.
The formula for speed of sound uses temperature incorrectly in the numerical example (T reported as 294,21 K rather than 294,06 K); ensure consistency in values.
The hypothesis restates f=Î»v but does not derive the predicted inverse Lâf relationship; include a brief derivation using Î»=4L/n to strengthen the hypothesis section.
The dependent variable section duplicates the independent variable explanation; revise to clearly describe resonance length measurement as separate from frequency control.
Speed of sound is listed as a controlled variable but is only calculated post-experiment; clarify how you will keep it constant during trials or treat it as a derived parameter instead.
Step 5 in the procedure (âspecific frequencies of 200 intervalsâ) is ambiguous; specify the exact frequency range and increments to improve reproducibility.
Identifying resonance by listening for louder sound is subjective; consider using a decibel meter or sensor to record intensity peaks for objective detection.
Qualitative data description is clear but would benefit from quantitative noise and signal-to-noise measurements to support claims about ambient conditions.
Converted data table shows inconsistent use of integral symbol in header instead of frequency f; correct the header to avoid confusion.
The uncertainty propagation formula incorrectly applies a factor 14 in ÎÎ» computation instead of 4; revise âÎ»/âL = 4/n and recalculate uncertainties.
Temperature uncertainty is ignored in the error propagation for speed of sound; include ÎT to provide a complete uncertainty analysis.
The conclusion quotes a theoretical 860 Hz value as matching experimental data, but this value does not come from your measurements; refer instead to the average experimental frequency.
Percentage error or quantitative comparison to theoretical values is missing; including error percentages for harmonics would strengthen the conclusionâs scientific context.
Evaluation identifies human error but omits discussion of instrument calibration and measurement resolution; address how device precision impacted data.
Proposed use of Fourier analysis for resonance identification is realistic and well-linked to observed limitations, showing a clear route to reduce systematic errors.
The piston assembly is a key strength, enabling precise and systematic air column adjustments that enhance measurement reliability and control.
Appendix tables contain extensive duplication and garbled entries; streamline and remove redundant raw data to improve clarity and data navigation.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
